analogy: Think of "Respiration in Plants" through the chapter's own opening images: UNIT 5 The reductionist approach to study of life forms resulted in increasing use of physico-chemical concepts and techniques. Majority of these studies employed either surviving tissue model or straightaway cell- free systems. An explosion of knowledge resulted in molecular biology. Molecular physiology became almost... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: HUMAN PHYSIOLOGY, 183 BREATHING AND EXCHANGE OF GASES, 14.1 RESPIRATORY ORGANS, BREATHING AND EXCHANGE OF GASES. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Respiration in Plants is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that uNIT 5 The reductionist approach to study of life forms resulted in increasing use of physico-chemical concepts and techniques.

The text explains that majority of these studies employed either surviving tissue model or straightaway cell- free systems.

The text explains that an explosion of knowledge resulted in molecular biology.

The text explains that molecular physiology became almost synonymous with biochemistry and biophysics.

The text explains that however, it is now being increasingly realised that neither a purely organismic approach nor a purely reductionistic molecular approach would reveal the truth about biological processes or living phenomena.

The text explains that systems biology makes us believe that all living phenomena are emergent properties due to interaction among components of the system under study.

The text explains that regulatory network of molecules, supra molecular assemblies, cells, tissues, organisms and indeed, populations and communities, each create emergent properties.

The text explains that in the chapters under this unit, major human physiological processes like, exchange of gases, blood circulation, locomotion and movement are described in cellular and molecular terms.

The text explains that the last two chapters point to the coordination and regulation of body events at the organismic level.

The text explains that hUMAN PHYSIOLOGY Chapter 14 Breathing and Exchange of Gases Chapter 15 Body Fluids and Circulation Chapter 16 Excretory Products and their Elimination Chapter 17 Locomotion and Movement Chapter 18 Neural Control and Coordination Chapter 19 Chemical Coordination and Integration

The text explains that aLFONSO CORTI, Italian anatomist, was born in 1822.

The text explains that corti began his scientific career studying the cardiovascular systems of reptiles.

The text explains that later, he turned his attention to the mammalian auditory system.

The text explains that in 1851, he published a paper describing a structure located on the basilar membrane of the cochlea containing hair cells that convert sound vibrations into nerve impulses, the organ of Corti.

The text explains that 183 BREATHING AND EXCHANGE OF GASES As you have read earlier, oxygen (O2) is utilised by the organisms to indirectly break down simple molecules like glucose, amino acids, fatty acids, etc., to derive energy to perform various activities.

The text explains that carbon dioxide (CO2) which is harmful is also released during the above catabolic reactions.

The text explains that this process of exchange of O2 from the atmosphere with CO2 produced by the cells is called breathing, commonly known as respiration.

The text explains that the respiratory organs and the mechanism of breathing are described in the following sections of this chapter.

The text explains that earthworms use their moist cuticle and insects have a network of tubes (tracheal tubes) to transport atmospheric air within the body.

The text explains that special vascularised structures called gills (branchial respiration) are used by most of the aquatic arthropods and molluscs whereas vascularised bags called lungs (pulmonary respiration) are used by the terrestrial forms for the exchange of gases.

The text explains that bREATHING AND EXCHANGE OF GASES CHAPTER 14 14.1 Respiratory Organs 14.2 Mechanism of Breathing 14.3 Exchange of Gases 14.4 Transport of Gases 14.5 Regulation of Respiration 14.6 Disorders of Respiratory System

The text explains that 184 BIOLOGY 14.1.1 Human Respiratory System We have a pair of external nostrils opening out above the upper lips.

The text explains that trachea is a straight tube extending up to the mid-thoracic cavity, which divides at the level of 5th thoracic vertebra into a right and left primary bronchi.

The text explains that each terminal bronchiole gives rise to a number of very thin, irregular-walled and vascularised bag-like structures called alveoli.

The text explains that the outer pleural membrane is in close contact with the thoracic Bronchus Lung heart Diaphragm Epiglottis Larynx Trachea Cut end of rib Pleural membranes Alveoli Pleural fluid Bronchiole Figure 14.1 Diagrammatic view of human respiratory system (sectional view of the left lung is also shown)

The text explains that the part starting with the external nostrils up to the terminal bronchioles constitute the conducting part whereas the alveoli and their ducts form the respiratory or exchange part of the respiratory system.

The text explains that the conducting part transports the atmospheric air to the alveoli, clears it from foreign particles, humidifies and also brings the air to body temperature.

The text explains that such an arrangement is essential for breathing, as we cannot directly alter the pulmonary volume.

The text explains that (v) Utilisation of O2 by the cells for catabolic reactions and resultant release of CO2 (cellular respiration as dealt in the Chapter 12).

The text explains that the movement of air into and out of the lungs is carried out by creating a pressure gradient between the lungs and the atmosphere.

The text explains that inspiration can occur if the pressure within the lungs (intra-pulmonary pressure) is less than the atmospheric pressure, i.e., there is a negative pressure in the lungs with respect to atmospheric pressure.

The text explains that similarly, expiration takes place when the intra-pulmonary pressure is higher than the atmospheric pressure.

The text explains that a n increase in pulmonary volume decreases the intra- pulmonary pressure to less than the atmospheric pressure which forces the air from outside to move into the lungs, i.e., inspiration (Figure 14.2a).

The text explains that this leads to an increase in intra-pulmonary pressure to slightly above the atmospheric pressure causing the expulsion of air from the lungs, i.e., expiration (Figure 14.2b).

The text explains that o2 and CO2 are exchanged in these sites by simple diffusion mainly based on pressure/concentration gradient.

The text explains that pressure contributed by an individual gas in a mixture of gases is called partial pressure and is represented as pO2 for oxygen and pCO2 for carbon dioxide.

The text explains that partial pressures of these two gases in the atmospheric air and the two sites of diffusion are given in Table 14.1 and in Figure 14.3.

The text explains that similarly, TABLE 14.1 Partial Pressures (in mm Hg) of Oxygen and Carbon dioxide at Different Parts Involved in Diffusion in Comparison to those in Atmosphere Respiratory Atmospheric Alveoli Blood Blood Tissues Gas Air (Deoxygenated) (Oxygenated) O2 159 104 40 95 40 CO2 0.3 40 45 40 45

The text explains that 188 BIOLOGY a gradient is present for CO2 in the opposite direction, i.e., from tissues to blood and blood to alveoli.

The text explains that as the solubility of CO2 is 20-25 times higher than that of O2, the amount of CO2 that can diffuse through the diffusion membrane per unit difference in partial pressure is much higher compared to that of O2.

The text explains that binding of oxygen with haemoglobin is primarily related to partial pressure of O2.

The text explains that partial pressure of CO2, hydrogen ion concentration and temperature are the other factors which can interfere with this binding.

The text explains that what will be the pO2 and pCO2 in the atmospheric air compared to those in the alveolar air ?

The text explains that (i) pO2 lesser, pCO2 higher (ii) pO2 higher, pCO2 lesser (iii) pO2 higher, pCO2 higher (iv) pO2 lesser, pCO2 lesser 6.

The text explains that explain the process of inspiration under normal conditions.

The text explains that what is the effect of pCO2 on oxygen transport?

The text explains that what happens to the respiratory process in a man going up a hill?

The text explains that what is the site of gaseous exchange in an insect?

The text explains that can you suggest any reason for its sigmoidal pattern?

The text explains that try to gather information about it, and discuss with your friends.

The text explains that distinguish between (a) IRV and ERV (b) Inspiratory capacity and Expiratory capacity.

The text explains that find out the Tidal volume (approximate value) for a healthy human in an hour.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Biology, Chapter 14: Respiration in Plants

prelims_traps: Do not treat HUMAN PHYSIOLOGY and 183 BREATHING AND EXCHANGE OF GASES as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Respiration in Plants; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
